kjv_text: They shall come, and shall declare his righteousness unto a people that shall be born, that he hath done this.

thread.thread: Psalm 22's final line ("...that he hath done this") and Jesus' last word in John ("It is finished", John 19:30) land on the very same note: a finished saving work, a thousand years apart. This is thematic RESONANCE, not citation -- Hebrew asah ('done') and Greek tetelestai ('finished') are different words, and the short says so out loud. The cross is the completed work; nothing is left for you to earn -- come home to the One who said it is done.
thread.lever: overlooked detail / thematic resonance (Ps 22:31's accomplished saving work echoes John 19:30 'It is finished' -- resonance, NOT a verbal/etymological citation)
thread.anchor_detail: the psalm's closing line proclaiming God's righteous saving work accomplished and declared to a people not yet born -- echoing Christ's finished work on the cross
thread.why_fresh: the psalm and the cross are the two bookends of redemption, both closing on a finished work -- different words, in different tongues, a thousand years apart, but the same note
thread.gospel_landing: the whole work of bringing you home is complete; nothing left for you to finish; come home to the One who said 'It is finished'
thread.rationale: a faithful resonance (explicitly NOT a word-for-word citation) between Psalm 22's last line and Jesus' last word, with a grace landing that leaves nothing to earn

beats.text: A psalm's last line and Jesus' last breath in John close on the very same note: it is done.
The psalm closes looking ahead — to God's saving work, accomplished, and told to a people not yet born. Its final line: "…that he hath done this."
Done. And as Jesus hung dying, John records His final word: "It is finished".
Different words, in different tongues, a thousand years apart — but the same note: a finished work.
So what's left for you to finish? Nothing — only Someone to come home to: the One who said it is done.
